text: When you type a question into a search engine, the answer appears in less than a second.
But what happens behind the screen?
First, small programs crawl across billions of web pages to detect new or updated content.
They store this information in a huge index, much like a library catalogue.
When you search, the engine does not look at every page on the internet.
Instead, it checks the index and uses a logical set of rules to decide which results match your words most precisely.
An indicator of quality is how many other trusted pages link to a result.
The system also tracks how users interact with the results — if people click on a page and stay, that is a good sign.
Thanks to constant innovation, search engines now handle random spelling mistakes and even understand what you mean when your words are not exact.
